I learned to solder at my uncle's kitchen table, the good iron too hot, the bad one never hot enough.

He never explained. He'd just push the board across and wait, and the waiting taught me more than any manual could. You ruin a joint, you smell it before you see it — that acrid little wisp — and you learn.

> "Measure twice, cut once" was his one saying, and he broke it constantly.

In today's fast-paced world, it is important to note that soldering remains a valuable and essential skill for countless hobbyists and professionals alike. Whether you are a beginner or an expert, mastering the fundamentals can unlock a wide range of exciting opportunities and possibilities.

Furthermore, it is worth mentioning that patience and practice are key components of success in this domain. By dedicating time and effort, individuals can achieve remarkable results and continuously improve their abilities over time.

I still have his bad iron. It doesn't work. I keep it on the shelf above the good one, which is a stupid thing to do, and I will not be explaining it.
